data.title: ‘Tolerance Will Narrow’: Why Tesla Stock’s Price Target Got Lowered at Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) Q2 earnings report appears not to have gone down well with investors, with shares falling by 7% ahead of Thursday’s trading session.  

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

High conviction TSLA bears now have this Tradr ETF

The EV maker delivered a strong increase in revenue during the second quarter, but that growth came alongside a sharp deterioration in profitability. Tesla continues to spend aggressively on expanding its manufacturing footprint, developing new products and building out the AI infrastructure needed to support its autonomy and robotics ambitions.

Q2 revenue climbed 25.6% year-over-year to $28.24 billion, exceeding expectations by $1.75 billion. The company also surpassed $100 billion in trailing 12-month revenue for the first time.

The bottom line, however, was considerably weaker. Operating income plunged 57% to $398 million, sending the operating margin down to 1.4% from 4.1% a year earlier. Adjusted net income came in at $1.15 billion, equivalent to $0.33 per share, falling short of consensus estimates by $0.21 and declining from $0.40 in the year-ago quarter.

Tesla said the pressure on earnings was down to a combination of substantially higher operating expenses, a decline in regulatory credit revenue and lower average selling prices.

The company’s spending also accelerated sharply. Capital expenditure jumped 142% YoY to $5.8 billion, pushing free cash flow into negative territory at $1.1 billion. Tesla now expects 2026 capex to exceed $25 billion, compared with $8.5 billion in 2025, and has warned that spending will continue to increase over the next two to three years as it invests in AI and robotics.

Morgan Stanley’s Andrew Percoco, an analyst who ranks among the top 2% on Wall Street, believes the spending outlook is becoming an increasingly important consideration for investors. He argues that the planned capex expansion is necessary if Tesla is to “establish and defend” a leading position in autonomous driving and robotics, but the market will ultimately want to see evidence that those investments are producing returns. “Specifically,” he said, “a scaled robotaxi network that demonstrates increasing density and improving safety within existing cities, plus tangible progress commercializing Optimus.”

The analyst has also become more cautious about Tesla’s near-term earnings trajectory. Percoco expects weaker gross margins in the automotive and energy segments to persist through the remainder of the year, while rising R&D expenses should create additional pressure. Accordingly, he has reduced his 2026 adjusted EBITDA forecast by 7%.

The pressure is expected to continue into 2027, with Percoco anticipating further R&D investment in areas including Optimus and semiconductor manufacturing, while energy margins are expected to remain subdued. As such, Percoco has lowered his 2027 adjusted EBITDA forecast by 12%.

The most significant revision, however, relates to capital expenditure. The analyst now expects Tesla to spend almost $30 billion in 2027, up from a previous estimate of $20 billion. Combined with the lower EBITDA forecast, this implies free cash flow burn of approximately $14 billion, compared with Percoco’s previous estimate of $5 billion.

“Absent consistent, transparent proof points, we’d expect the market’s tolerance for incremental capex to narrow,” the 5-star analyst said.

To this end, reflecting “increasing capex and worsening cash burn through the end of the decade,” Percoco has lowered his price target from $417 to $400, although the new figure still implies the stock will gain 14.5% from current levels. Percoco’s rating stays an Equal-weight (i.e., Neutral). (To watch Percoco’s track record, click here)

15 other analysts join Percoco on the sidelines, while an additional 8 Buys and 3 Sells all coalesce to a Hold consensus rating. The $397.05 average price target closely resembles Percoco’s objective. (See Tesla stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Reddit Stock Sinks on News of Google Rift – Here’s What Analysts Make of It
data.description:
Wednesday was a bad day for Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) stock. Shares fell by 8% after The Wall Street Journal reported that the company may restrict Google parent Alphabet’s access to its content for AI-related purposes as a $60 million-per-year licensing agreement approaches expiration.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

The report said Reddit is weighing whether to limit Google’s access to its platform for AI training or seek a substantially higher renewal fee. Other publishers, including USA Today, Reuters, and Politico, are likewise reassessing Google’s web crawler as AI-generated summaries reduce the traffic sent to their websites. The Wall Street Journal attributed its report to people familiar with the matter, while Reddit has not publicly commented.

The development follows a Digiday report last week that cited an executive discussing a similar possibility and broadly aligns with RBC analyst Brad Erickson’s checks, which suggested that Reddit was being pushed to introduce content and account-quality controls to better accommodate Google’s core and spam updates in May and June, respectively.

On the whole, Erickson views the situation as negative for Reddit, arguing that investor expectations had risen in recent months that the company would secure a favourable outcome with Google and potentially announce it at an upcoming earnings report, making this a “significant step backwards.” The analyst says content quality has long been a challenge, with the rapid expansion of AI-generated content making the issue more difficult as Google continues to evolve its algorithms and AI content standards.

While Reddit retains leverage through its leading share of citations in AI Overviews, Erickson notes that the company has been seeking a variable compensation arrangement or increased traffic rather than simply renewing its existing fixed $60 million annual payment, while Google has strong incentives to limit content-cost inflation and recognises its significant influence over Reddit’s traffic and valuation.

Ultimately, the analyst believes the uncertainty surrounding Google’s long-term AI strategy makes it difficult to negotiate guarantees around future traffic, as even Google might not yet know how its AI search and broader internet strategy will evolve.

All told, Erickson maintained a Sector Perform (i.e., Neutral) rating on the shares, although that might as well be a Buy, given his $250 price target suggests shares will climb 47% higher in the year ahead. (To watch Erickson’s track record, click here)

D.A. Davidson analyst Wyatt Swanson also believes the WSJ report is significant given investors had expected details of Reddit’s Google partnership renewal to emerge in the coming quarters. At the same time, recent traffic deceleration and Reddit’s apparent testing of measures to push mobile web users toward its app suggest the company may be preparing to reduce its reliance on Google-driven referral traffic.

Although this could pose a risk to future DAUq (Daily Active Uniques) growth, particularly among logged-out users, the analyst does not believe investors should assume the partnership will simply end, noting that both companies have incentives to find a way to preserve referral traffic while accommodating Google’s AI search.

Swanson also argues that Reddit’s largely human-generated content gives it meaningful leverage in negotiations, as its data is particularly valuable to AI models, while the company could potentially offset weaker search-driven traffic by increasing direct user engagement.

All told, Swanson maintained a Buy rating on the shares, while his $200 price target implies the stock will post growth of 17% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Swanson’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the stock claims an additional 12 Buys, 6 Holds and 1 Sell, for a Moderate Buy consensus view. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 30%, considering the average target clocks in at $222. (See RDDT stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Hold Your Horses,’ Says Wedbush About Meta Stock Ahead of Earnings
data.description:
This Q2 earnings season is all about hyperscaler spending plans. Combined AI investment by major platforms is expected to approach $725 billion in 2026, putting growing pressure on FCF (free cash flow) and turning investors’ attention from the infrastructure buildout to the profitability of those investments.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

As such, with Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) set to deliver its Q2 readout next Wednesday, July 29, its CapEx plans will be closely watched, with the company having seen significant growth on this front.

Wedbush analyst Ygal Arounian expects that trend to continue as the company pursues its Superintelligence ambitions and expands its Muse Spark foundation models.

While Arounian does not anticipate specific 2027 CapEx guidance, he believes Meta could raise its 2026 outlook again and signal that spending will increase materially in 2027. His estimates and consensus estimates currently call for 2026 CapEx of $138 billion and $139 billion, respectively – both slightly above the midpoint of management’s $125 billion to $145 billion guidance range. For 2027, Arounian estimates CapEx of $173 billion, broadly in line with consensus at $175 billion, although he believes buy-side expectations are already above $200 billion and could rise some more. The spending trajectory could determine whether Meta’s FCF turns negative in 2026 or 2027, compared with Arounian’s current expectation of “slightly positive.”

Arounian will also be watching the health of Meta’s advertising business and the benefits generated by AI. AI-driven improvements have become a “clear lever,” with advances in Andromeda’s compute efficiency, Lattice modeling and the GEM model architecture already delivering benefits. Business AI is also making early progress, although it has yet to be directly monetized. “We look for updates on continued progress, and ability to drive further outsized gains in impression and pricing growth,” the analyst said.

Arounian views Meta’s Business AI initiatives as one of the company’s more advanced AI monetization opportunities outside of advertising improvements. Meta launched the Meta Business Agent and Meta Business Agent Platform at Conversations 2026 in June, offering businesses AI agents that operate across WhatsApp, Messenger and Instagram. The tools can handle customer inquiries, product recommendations, appointment bookings, lead qualification and sales conversion.  

The next phase of Meta’s AI strategy and monetization efforts will also be important. Longer term, Arounian believes opportunities could include selling excess computing capacity (as recently reported), expanding subscription tiers, and potentially licensing Muse Spark to enterprise and consumer users or offering API access.

Summing up, Arounian takes a balanced view of the social media giant’s prospects. “The gap between capex intensity and diversified monetization remains the central debate for the stock (although strength in the core ad business is certainly a positive), in our view, and the reason we remain on the sidelines despite Meta’s valuation discount to peers,” the analyst summed up.

To this end, Arounian maintained a Neutral (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, backed by a $671 price target, a figure suggesting shares will gain 6.5% in the year ahead. (To watch Arounian’s track record, click here)

Most of Arounian’s colleagues have a more upbeat stance. All in, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 35 Buys and 5 Holds. Going by the $805.98 average price target, a year from now, shares will be changing hands for a 28% premium. (See Meta stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Super Micro Computer Stock Will Head Higher, Says Rosenblatt (If History Repeats)
data.description:
Wednesday is shaping up to be a strong day for Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) investors, with shares jumping 14% in pre-market trading after the AI server maker released preliminary guidance for its fiscal fourth quarter.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

Supermicro expects FQ4 revenue to come in near the low end of its $11 billion to $12.5 billion guidance range, compared with consensus expectations of $11.73 billion. While that may initially appear disappointing, the company also expects GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins to reach between 15% and 17%, well above its previous guidance of 8.2% to 8.4%. The improvement is down to a favorable customer and product mix.

The company also reported that backlog reached record levels at the end of fiscal 2026, with more than $60 billion in new orders received during the fourth quarter.

Rosenblatt analyst Sajal Dogra believes the scale of the Q4 order book further highlights SMCI’s industry-leading time-to-market advantage and “unique ability” to capitalize on the AI infrastructure buildout.

“Remarkably,” the analyst went on to say, “this implies orders increased by over 50% in just one month, after management disclosed $39B of new orders from over 20 customers and raised capital specifically to procure components needed to fulfill that backlog.”

Dogra argues that the record order book is not even the most important takeaway. The gross margin guide of 16% exceeds the peak achieved during the Hopper cycle, offering the “clearest evidence yet” that SMCI’s integrated platform strategy – including DLC (direct liquid cooling), rack-scale integration, software and services, and customer diversification – is “structurally expanding” margins, although some component cost timing benefits may also be contributing this quarter.

With DLC capacity still supply constrained and rack ASPs (average selling prices) expected to increase from roughly $3 million to $8 million as Vera Rubin systems ramp, the analyst believes the debate has “shifted from demand to execution.”

Key factors now include supply availability, the pace of manufacturing, and whether customers are ready to deploy the systems. Dogra estimates that every 100 basis points of gross margin translates into approximately $0.40 to $0.45 of annualized EPS.

Prior to today’s uptick, the share price had almost halved since early June, and with a recent capital raise in the rear-view mirror, Dogra views the recent pullback as an “attractive entry point.”

The analyst points to previous examples of mandatory convertible preferred share issuances in the semiconductor industry, which can result in lower dilution when share prices are higher than would be the case with a straightforward equity offering. Those instances ultimately proved to be “fantastic buying opportunities,” and Dogra expects SMCI to “follow a similar path.”

Bottom line, Dogra maintained a Buy rating on the shares and raised his price target from $40 to $45, based on an 11x multiple of estimated FY27 non-GAAP EPS, suggesting the stock will gain 53% over the coming months. “The last time SMCI consistently generated gross margins in the 13 – 14% range, the shares traded at a low-teens forward P/E multiple, suggesting further upside ahead,” Dogra summed up.

3 other analysts join Dogra in the bull camp, yet with an additional 9 Holds and 1 Sell, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating. The average price target stands at $37.91, suggesting share appreciation of 29% in the year ahead. (See SMCI stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: China, Share Buybacks, US 1 List: Why Bank of America Is Pounding the Table on Micron Stock
data.description:
After undergoing a steep selloff, Micron (NASDAQ:MU) appears to be regaining its footing. The stock advanced on Monday and looks poised to extend those gains in Tuesday’s trading.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

The recent weakness reflected several factors, including a broader tech-sector selloff, concerns that AI infrastructure spending could begin to cool, and fears that Chinese memory-chip manufacturers such as CXMT could intensify competitive pressure.

The selloff now appears to have eased, and with big tech about to report Q2 earnings, we will soon get a clearer picture regarding the second concern. As for the competitive threat from China, BofA analyst Vivek Arya, who ranks among the top 2% of analysts on Wall Street, does not view CXMT as a significant threat.

Although the China-based company is “aggressively expanding” its production capacity, reaching the low-teens percentage range of global wafer capacity, Arya sees “limited competition” in AI memory. CXMT mainly caters to the underserved consumer and commodity DRAM markets rather than HBM3E and HBM4. It also remains uncertain whether U.S. original equipment manufacturers will receive government approval to source from CXMT in the near term.

Meanwhile, Arya also thinks that Chinese open LLMs are “no threat to memory demand.”

In fact, the latest wave of open-source model releases, including China’s Kimi K3, launched on July 16, reinforces the analyst’s “bullish thesis” on memory and Micron. Arya argues that the aggressive pricing of Chinese open-model APIs – which can be up to 5–350 times cheaper than Western alternatives – reflects business-model decisions rather than necessarily lower hardware costs. Although these models employ various efficiency techniques to reduce compute and GPU intensity, they still require the same or greater memory as model weights and active parameters expand. Arya also points out that every open-weight download creates a new customer-side memory socket that would not exist in a closed-model environment.

Separately, Arya also sees another catalyst ahead. Micron’s restrictions on share buybacks under the CHIPS Act are expected to expire around December 2026, two years after the company received its first funding tranche in December 2024. That could pave the way for large-scale repurchases, particularly given the company’s projected free cash flow of b$120 billion–$130 billion annually over the next few years. A 40% payout policy would translate into approximately $50 billion–$60 billion in annual buybacks, equivalent to roughly 5%–6% of Micron’s ~$1 trillion market capitalization each year.

Lastly, further adding to bullish sentiment, Micron, alongside Keurig Dr Pepper, has been added to BofA’s US 1 List. The list is designed to feature the firm’s strongest investment ideas from among Buy-rated, U.S.-listed stocks.

Bottom line, Arya maintained a Buy rating on MU shares, along with a $1,550 price objective, implying the stock will gain 79% over the next year. (To watch Arya’s track record, click here)

Arya’s objective is only slightly lower than the Street’s $1,569.29 average price target, a figure that calls for 12-month returns of 81%. On the rating front, Micron gets a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a lopsided mix of 29 Buys and 1 Hold. (See Micron stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Meta and Shopify: Analyst Lifts Price Target on One Stock but Downgrades the Other
data.description:
Meta’s (NASDAQ:META) new AI-driven opportunity is about to cause a lot of trouble for Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP).

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

So says Rothschild analyst Dominic Ball, who believes Meta’s launch of a “one-prompt entrepreneur” will make life very difficult for the e-commerce platform.

The analyst argues that Meta’s central strategic question is how to deploy its capital and computing resources. While the company’s spending is still viewed by many investors through the lens of its metaverse ambitions, Ball believes Meta’s gen AI investments could ultimately produce a very different outcome.

He expects Meta’s returns on capital to inflect as the company potentially shifts from developing a consumer AI assistant toward an AI platform for small businesses. Ball believes an SMB-focused large language model could provide Meta with a “longer growth runway, wider moat, and higher margins and returns.” He also expects any excess computing capacity to be sold to a diversified customer base through an AI cloud business, rather than being concentrated among a small number of customers.

Ball forecasts a 45% IRR (internal rate of return) for the potential SMB AI business, which he believes would exceed the returns generated by both Meta’s core advertising business and AI cloud business. Ultimately, he argues that the opportunity comes down to capital allocation, with Meta likely to pursue the highest-return option, as it did during its 2023 “year of efficiency.”

Essentially, Ball sees the company eventually developing a “one-prompt entrepreneur” SMB AI platform capable of building an e-commerce storefront, creating and managing advertising campaigns, handling payments through providers such as Stripe, and managing catalogues and customer relationship management systems on an end-to-end basis. Ball argues that Meta would have two major advantages: its distribution, with approximately 250 million businesses already using its platforms, and the ability to offer advertising with higher returns on ad spend.

According to Ball, a shift toward an SMB AI platform could unlock a realistic revenue opportunity of more than $209 billion outside China and drive an inflection in Meta’s ROIC (return on invested capital). He also argues that regulation is strengthening rather than weakening Meta’s core business by widening its moat, reducing competition, and supporting structural margins. “We view Meta as the modern-day Philip Morris, which outperformed the S&P 500 index by over 1,000bp pa from the 1960s to 2000 as it was steeply regulated,” Ball added. “Meta’s social media runway remains long – c30% more time spent on the platforms – due to strengthening cultural, structural, chemical and psychological tailwinds.”

Ball does add that such a product has not been confirmed or launched.

Nevertheless, along with maintaining a Buy rating on Meta shares, Ball raised his price target from $900 to $1,000, suggesting the stock could gain 55% in the months ahead. (To watch Ball’s track record, click here)

However, Ball believes the potential Meta AI threat warrants a more cautious stance on Shopify.

In his view, Shopify’s “largest and most overlooked growth engine” – same-store sales growth driven by accelerating social commerce trends – could shift from a tailwind to a headwind if Meta eventually launches the SMB LLM. Ball believes Meta’s bigger brand, broader distribution, and ability to offer higher returns on advertising spend could allow it to capture incremental SMB customers in an online e-commerce market characterised by high churn rates, where Shopify currently holds the “incumbent position.”

He argues that Shopify’s international and enterprise expansion would not be sufficient to offset the potential threat. As a result, Ball reduced his estimates for Shopify’s future SMB share gains and with a “heavy heart,” he has downgraded the stock to Neutral. He also slashed the price target from $160 to $130, suggesting the stock will gain a modest 4% over the next year.

Ball’s colleagues only partially agree with his stance as both stocks claim Strong Buy consensus ratings. META’s $805.98 average price target implies one-year share appreciation of 25%, while SHOP’s $158.30 average target makes room for 12-month returns of 27%. (See META stock forecast or SHOP stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Intel Will Beat Expectations in Q2, Says Top Analyst – That Doesn’t Make the Stock a Buy
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), like many of its high-flying chip peers, has been hit hard by the recent chip stocks selloff.  Even after factoring in Monday’s sector-wide bounce, the shares currently sit 30% below the peaks reached only a month ago, as the market has become more cautious about elevated valuations amid fears of an AI bubble.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

So, with the company slated to report Q2 earnings on Thursday (July 23), Wedbush’s Matt Bryson, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, thinks it is “not whether Intel beats expectations, but rather how does sentiment shift.”

With revenue and margins appearing likely to significantly exceed Q2 expectations, alongside a comparable outlook heading into CQ3 – which could also receive a boost from operating expense cuts following another round of workforce reductions – Bryson expects numbers will “move meaningfully higher.”

However, as demonstrated by TSM, even a sizeable earnings beat accompanied by accelerating sales growth was insufficient to overcome a semiconductor and hardware sector sell-off driven by “amorphous concerns.”

These included fears around China’s progress in AI (such as Moonshot), wider macroeconomic pressures (including heightened Iran-US tensions and inflation’s potential impact on future US interest rate decisions), and uncertainty surrounding data center spending (including hyperscaler returns on investment and project delays). “And while Intel may be better positioned to deal with at least one of these worries (as China still needs compute to support inference), with Intel’s valuation well above historical norms (and industry peers), we also believe it is arguably more susceptible to broader market swings (unlike say TSM or NVDA),” the 5-star analyst said.

Nevertheless, Bryson sees the results exceeding consensus expectations, driven by several factors. Intel’s guidance implies just over 5% sequential revenue growth, with the Street expecting a similar increase in Q3, but Bryson estimates data center sales could rise around 10% quarter-over-quarter and 40% year-over-year.

With server ASPs already increasing by double digits in Q2 following earlier price hikes, and additional increases reportedly planned for Q3, pricing alone could account for much of the expected growth. Further upside could come from improved production as Intel ramps 18A, allowing older nodes to be redirected toward server demand.

PC CPU pricing is also providing support, with increases appearing broadly in line with server CPUs. This could help justify Intel’s modest sequential PC revenue growth expectations despite weaker PC demand.

Meanwhile, Intel’s margin guidance appears conservative, factoring in the loss of a one-time benefit from selling previously scrapped inventory and the costs associated with the 18A ramp. However, continued pricing strength and faster-than-expected yield improvements across both 18A and legacy processes could drive gross margins ahead of expectations and create further upside into future quarters.

While Bryson’s preview is a positive one, he evidently thinks the goodness is already priced in.

Ahead of the print, he maintained a Neutral rating on the shares although he might as well have said Sell, given his $60 price target points toward a 12-month decline of 39%. (To watch Bryson’s track record, click here)

The analyst consensus also rates the stock a Hold, based on a mix of 24 Holds, 10 Buys and 2 Sells. However, unlike Bryson’s low target, the $113.72 average price target points toward 12-month returns of 16%. (See INTC stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Microsoft and ServiceNow: One Beaten-Down Software Stock Is a Buy, the Other Is a Sell, Says Analyst
data.description:
Investors have gotten jittery regarding the prospects of a software industry faced with the disruptive nature of AI. In a post-AI landscape, software will need to evolve, given the risk of disruption will likely be persistent.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools with TipRanks Premium to make smarter, more confident investment decisions
  • Subscribe to TipRanks Smart Investor Newsletter, and discover new investing opportunities with data-backed stock picks

Yet, CLSA analyst Bhavtosh Vajpayee argues that the “death of software” is an “overrated fear.”

His bottom-up analysis highlights the complexity of real-world business processes, where software plays a critical role in managing operational nuances, compliance requirements, and information flows. The idea that AI could simply replace these systems through “vibe coding” appears unrealistic.

While Vajpayee sees “moats everywhere” across the software sector, he believes investors still “need to watch their backs,” as some companies offer more compelling setups than others.

Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) have both been pressured by the broader selloff in software stocks, but Vajpayee argues that only one currently has a sufficiently strong investment case.

Looking at Microsoft, the bear case rests on the idea that its legacy strengths may not fully translate into the AI era. Despite its enterprise dominance, the company lacks a leading AI model, faces uncertainty around Copilot, and could lose software pricing power as AI agents make traditional tools easier to replicate. Azure could also face increasing commoditization as competition intensifies and AI infrastructure demands rise, putting pressure on margins and making its ~20x forward earnings valuation appear expensive.

However, the bulls have a case too, claiming that Microsoft’s enterprise reach remains a powerful AI advantage. Its massive user base, trusted relationships, and ecosystem could make it the key platform for AI adoption, even without owning the top AI models. Supporters argue its growth, cash flows, and valuation remain attractive, with AI providing significant upside despite current execution challenges.

What stance does Vajpayee take? “We have chosen the optimistic path with Microsoft,” says the analyst. “We are not apologists for Microsoft’s AI stumbles yet see the 25% drop from recent peaks as harsh amid turmoil in SaaS stocks. Microsoft is more than SaaS, we contend. As a foundational layer to tech, we see low risk of obsolescence, although more must be done to grab the upside.”

Accordingly, Vajpayee initiated coverage of MSFT stock with a Buy rating and $535 price target, implying the shares will gain 36% over the coming months. (To watch Vajpayee’s track record, click here)

Assessing the case of ServiceNow, the analyst argues that it has remained remarkably resilient despite growing concerns that AI could disrupt the software industry. Unlike many SaaS companies, ServiceNow’s financial performance has stayed largely intact, with FY26 guidance pointing to continued subscription growth of 22.5% and non-GAAP operating margins of 31.5%.

As a platform serving IT departments – a function expected to be at the centre of AI adoption – ServiceNow’s ability to withstand early disruption suggests it has “stickiness that begets differentiation.”

However, Vajpayee believes that the stock’s steep decline is understandable, as it reflects the “worst of the vagaries of SaaS.” Even after falling more than half from its 2024 peak, ServiceNow still trades at elevated valuations of around 37x December 2027 GAAP earnings and 23x non-GAAP earnings. Additional concerns include heavy stock-based compensation, recent acquisition activity, and the risk that recurring AI-driven product improvements could eventually weaken its competitive position.

“The point is – ServiceNow has done well, but for the risks, it is still overpriced,” Vajpayee summed up. “Despite our love for the Hoosiers – ServiceNow’s founder is one – we are initiating coverage with an Underperform (i.e., Sell) recommendation.”

That Underperform rating is backed by a $72 price target, implying the stock is still overvalued by 30%.

Vajpayee’s colleagues only partially agree with his takes, as both stocks claim Strong Buy consensus ratings. Microsoft’s average price target stands at $558.86, suggesting shares will climb 42% over the coming months. Meanwhile, ServiceNow’s $139.25 average target implies the stock will gain 35% in the year ahead. (See MSFT stock forecast or See NOW stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Top Analyst Christopher Rolland Raises Intel Stock’s Price Target Ahead of Earnings – Here’s What He’s Looking For
data.description:
After spending the first half of the year soaring to giddy heights, semiconductor stocks have more recently been experiencing quite a selloff. Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) was a major participant in the uptick and, accordingly, has featured in the pullback. Shares have retreated by 32% from the highs notched only last month.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

With the chip giant slated to release Q2 earnings next Thursday (July 23), investors will be hoping the print can help build momentum again.

Looking ahead to the readout, Susquehanna’s Christopher Rolland, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, is expecting better Q2 results, driven mainly by improved server CPU performance and, to a lesser extent, healthier PC ODM builds. However, he expects PC builds in the second half of 2026 to come in well below normal seasonal levels due to the “worsening memory dynamics.”

Rolland also notes that Intel is likely to continue allocating its constrained front-end wafer capacity toward server processors to capitalize on the recent resurgence in demand for agentic AI CPUs. As such, Rolland models CCG (Client Computing Group) significantly below seasonal norms during what is typically the strongest period of the year.

On the server side, the analyst anticipates a robust Q2 and continued strength thereafter as the TAM (total addressable market) expands. That said, Rolland points out that Intel previously warned of “meaningful” supply constraints that could limit upside, and his recent channel checks indicate the server supply-demand imbalance might persist well into 2028. In response, Intel has announced an expansion of its Ireland fabrication facility to increase Intel 3 capacity, which Rolland views as a positive step toward meeting demand. “While we believe upside will ultimately depend on the pace of incremental capacity, we are modeling DCAI growth of 38% YOY in 2026,” the 5-star analyst added.

As for the foundry business, the analyst notes that management has previously sounded optimistic about advanced packaging, citing annual demand worth several billion dollars. Here, Rolland’s latest industry checks were “particularly constructive,” with one expert highlighting strong prospects for EMIB technology, including the possibility that Google could adopt EMIB-T advanced packaging for its next-generation TPUs. Regarding Intel’s 14A process, he says management remains optimistic, expecting customer design commitments to begin emerging in the second half of 2026.

On profitability, Rolland expects margins to be “broadly in line” with the guide, which calls for a 200-basis-point quarter-over-quarter decline. Looking ahead, while higher pricing for server processors should provide some support, he remains cautious about the potential for meaningful gross margin expansion, as Panther Lake and the 18A process are expected to remain dilutive through the end of the year. He also notes that Intel’s packaging business is still margin-dilutive and will continue to weigh on profitability as it scales.

“In short,” Rolland summed up, “we expect expanding Server demand to support upside in 2Q and beyond, more than offsetting possibly weaker PC builds.”

As such, Rolland maintained a Neutral rating on the shares, although his price target goes from $80 to $115. Should the figure be met, investors will be pocketing returns of 20% a year from now. (To watch Rolland’s track record, click here)

Most other analysts also remain on the INTC sidelines. Based on a total of 26 Holds, 10 Buys, and 2 Sells, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating. Going by the $109.29 average price target, shares will climb 14% higher over the coming months. (See INTC stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Not Cheap Enough,’ Says Top Investor About SpaceX Stock
data.description:
SpaceX’s (NASDAQ:SPCX) post-IPO meltdown continues. Shares of Elon Musk’s space company look primed for another down day in Friday’s session after the stock closed below the IPO price on Thursday.  

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

High conviction on SPCX? Try Tradr's SPCM or SPCG

The latest decline followed SpaceX postponing Starship’s 13th test flight on July 16 from Starbase, Texas, after engine issues triggered an automatic abort. The mission, which would have been the first to carry a Starlink payload, was expected to deploy 20 satellites to test extended solar arrays, antennas, and high-capacity laser links before the spacecraft re-entered the atmosphere. The flight plan mirrored previous tests, with the booster landing in the Gulf of Mexico and the upper stage completing a global flight before splashing down in the Indian Ocean. CEO Elon Musk said SpaceX aims to attempt another launch within days.

The latest pullback means the stock has erased 45% from its post-IPO high of $225.64, reflecting growing investor concerns over an elevated valuation, continued losses, and capital commitments tied to the xAI funding effort following a major bond offering.

But does the sharp drop now offer a big opportunity to “buy the dip”? Not at all, says top investor James Foord.

In his view, SpaceX remains “significantly overvalued” even after the selloff. At around $124 per share, the company trades at roughly 64× trailing sales based on 2025 revenue of about $18.7 billion, while even a stronger 2026 growth outlook would still leave the stock valued at nearly 46× forward sales.

The 5-star investor argues a large premium would only be justified by much faster growth and a clear path to expanding margins, but SpaceX’s revenue growth slowed to 33% in 2025. The valuation also sits well above both space-sector peers and major technology companies.

Meanwhile, the AI business added through the xAI deal remains a “cash-burn story” layered onto an already capital-heavy launch and connectivity operation. While Starlink continues to grow, pressure from lower ARPU (average revenue per user) as expansion shifts toward more affordable international markets remains a headwind.

So, at what point will it be worth wading in here? Well, somewhere quite below the current valuation. While 2026 revenue could reach around $39 billion on continued Starlink expansion, stronger launch activity, and early AI revenue, the current price implies roughly 46× forward sales, which is still difficult to justify.

Although SpaceX merits a premium over slower-growing tech giants, the investor argues the valuation should be closer to 20–25× sales given moderating growth, ongoing cash burn in the newest segment, and potential share supply. A 24× multiple on 2026 revenue would imply a market cap of about $930 billion, or roughly $70 per share. “Accounting for some Musk-like premium, I could justify starting to nibble closer to $90, but I won’t be touching SpaceX above $100,” Foord went on to say.

That still factors in a 19% drop from current levels. As such, Foord rates SPCX stock a Sell. (To watch Foord’s track record, click here)

Among the Street’s analysts, only one shares that bearish view, while an additional 23 Buys and 5 Holds all add up to a Strong Buy consensus rating. Meanwhile, the $243.81 average price target implies the stock will gain 95% over the coming months. (See SPCX stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Take This as a Warning,’ Says Investor About Nvidia Stock
data.description:
Despite chip stocks’ recent recent selloff, the segment got a positive signal this week. While TSMC’s strong Q2 results and outlook failed to boost currently damaged AI sentiment, the print offered assurance AI-driven demand hasn’t wavered at all.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

200% short exposure to NVDA with NVDS

In particular, says an investor known as Summit Research (SR), the readout reinforces the bullish case for chip king Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA).

As Nvidia’s primary wafer supplier and a leading indicator of AI infrastructure investment trends, TSMC increased its 2026 capital spending forecast, pointing to “stronger and stronger” AI demand that remains “difficult to immediately quantify.” More significantly, says SR, the company is seeing a rapid shift in its revenue mix toward advanced 3 nm process technology and its HPC (high-performance computing) platform.

TSMC reported Q2 revenue growth of 36% year-over-year to $40.2 billion, with HPC-related sales accounting for 66% of revenue, up from 61% in the previous quarter. Meanwhile, 3 nm technology reached 30% of revenue, compared with 25% in Q1 2026.

The shift toward 3 nm process technology and HPC is important for Nvidia because these reflect the manufacturing capabilities and end-market demand tied to its most advanced AI chips. Nvidia’s next-generation accelerators, including its Vera Rubin platform, rely on cutting-edge process nodes to deliver greater performance and efficiency, while TSMC’s HPC segment reflects demand for high-performance computing products such as AI data center processors.

As TSMC’s revenue mix moves increasingly toward 3 nm and HPC, it indicates that production capacity is being allocated toward advanced AI chips rather than lower-value applications. For Nvidia, this provides evidence that its supply chain is scaling to support the continued rollout of its next-generation platforms and suggests that demand for its AI infrastructure remains robust.

Taken together, TSMC’s latest earnings report strengthens the case that Nvidia’s growth outlook is “increasingly skewed to the upside.” The faster expansion of 3 nm (N3) production, rising HPC contribution, and significant increase in capital spending provide concrete support for a smooth Vera Rubin rollout while offering greater confidence in Nvidia’s ability to convert accelerating agentic into sustained long-term revenue growth.

“More importantly,” SR summed up, “the outperformance and implied demand dynamics may represent a warning shot to investors that Nvidia’s current discount will not persist for long. This accordingly supports prospects of a further re-rate higher from current levels – especially as improving supply visibility and accelerating infrastructure demand are increasingly reflected in Nvidia’s forward estimates.”

To this end, Summit Research rates NVDA stock a Buy. (To watch Summit Research’s track record, click here)

That is also the prevalent mood on Wall Street. Based on 36 Buys and 1 Hold, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Strong Buy. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 49%, considering the average target clocks in at $309.94. (See Nvidia stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: AMD’s Advancing AI Event Is Coming – Expect Big Announcements, Says Jefferies
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) is set to report Q2 earnings on August 4, when investors will be closely watching the outlook for its data center business to gauge whether demand for AI accelerators and EPYC server processors is strong enough to support the bull case.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

Before then, however, investors have another potential catalyst on the calendar. AMD’s Advancing AI conference will take place next week in San Francisco on July 22–23.

The event marks AMD’s first dedicated AI showcase since last summer, when it officially launched the MI350 series and offered the first preview of its Helios rack architecture.

Looking ahead, Jefferies analyst Blayne Curtis, who ranks among the top 1% of Wall Street analysts, expects several significant announcements.

For one, he expects the CPU TAM to increase above the $200 billion figure Nvidia cited in May.

Curtis also expects additional details on the MI500 GPU family and AMD’s scale-up roadmap, including the potential for optical to “enter the fold.”

AMD first previewed the MI500 series at CES 2026, revealing that the CDNA 6-based accelerators will be built on an advanced 2nm process, feature HBM4E memory, and launch in 2027. The company claimed the platform could deliver a 1,000x AI performance improvement vs. an eight-GPU MI300X node.

Curtis expects the MI500 platform to transition to a native UAL scale-up architecture supporting up to 256 GPUs per rack, a move that could require optical interconnects. The analyst will be watching to see whether AMD commits to a CPO (co-packaged optics)-based scale-up solution and who and who its optical engine partner is.

On the networking side, Curtis believes the company is working with Astera Labs on UAL and Broadcom on ESUN switches. Confirmation that optical interconnects will be used in the MI500 platform would be a meaningful positive for the optical supply chain. While Curtis is uncertain which CPO vendor AMD will ultimately select, he notes that the company is both an investor in and partner of Ayar Labs.

That said, as has been the case at the last two events, customer announcements are the “biggest swing factor.”

Beyond existing partners such as OpenAI and Meta, Curtis believes that based on his Asia supply chain checks, Microsoft is a customer for the upcoming MI450 platform. However, investor expectations are focused on the possibility of an Anthropic announcement.

Reports that Anthropic has been hiring engineers with ROCm expertise indicate the AI startup may be preparing to diversify its computing infrastructure.

That said, the economics of any Anthropic deal matter more than the headline itself. AMD has already committed substantial incentives to secure OpenAI and Meta as customers, so future agreements should require less generous terms. At current valuation levels, the market already expects OpenAI and Meta to each ramp to roughly 6GW of capacity. “A more traditional agreement with Anthropic would be a major positive for the stock and reinforce confidence in AMD’s ability to compete at the highest level without incentives,” the 5-star analyst summed up.

To this end, Curtis maintained a Buy rating on the shares, while his $515 price target suggests modest upside of 3% from current levels. (To watch Curtis’ track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the stock claims an additional 27 Buys and 8 Holds, for a Strong Buy consensus rating. Going by the $541.31 average price target, shares will add 8% in the months ahead. (See AMD stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Apple Stock Gets a Bullish Upgrade From HSBC – Here’s Why
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has famously been missing in action from the massive AI infrastructure buildouts its peers have undertaken, but it looks like investors are happy with that decision.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

The stock is currently the strongest-performing Mag 7 name this year, indicating that investors are beginning to appreciate that success in AI may not require developing the largest models, but rather controlling how consumers interact with the technology.

Now it seems that one Street analyst has also realized that Apple’s AI strategy could be about to pay off. HSBC analyst Nicolas Cote-Colisson upgraded Apple’s rating from Hold to Buy and raised the price target from $260 to $366, implying the stock will gain 10% over the coming months. (To watch Cote-Colisson’s track record, click here)

“Thus far, we had retained a cautious approach on Apple with a Hold rating,” said Cote-Colisson. “We had preferred other segments of the AI value chain, more prompt to exploit the bottlenecks created by the high demand in computing power, including hyperscalers or memory makers.”

But now Apple is at an “operational turning point,” with the analyst arguing that the company can avoid the heavy capital expenditure burden associated with AI infrastructure investment while benefiting from its massive ecosystem. Cote-Colisson noted that Apple is expected to invest only 2.5% of 2026 estimated sales in capital expenditure, compared with 39% for hyperscalers.

The analyst also believes that Apple is well positioned to leverage its 2.5 billion installed device base through its upcoming upgraded Apple Intelligence platform. “This AI boost comes at the right moment, when we think Apple has one of its most innovative product pipelines in place,” the analyst further added.

Apple’s new agentic Siri AI will launch this year, bringing features such as visual intelligence, cross-app information access for more context-aware conversations, and enhanced interactions.

Apple has so far struggled to deliver compelling use cases for Apple Intelligence, which was first introduced in June 2024. The company is now expected to rely on foundation models derived from distilled Gemini models, running both on devices and through Apple’s private cloud infrastructure.

At the same time, the company boasts a strong hardware roadmap, including the iPhone 18 Pro and Pro Max expected this autumn, the iPhone Air planned for April 2027, and, most importantly, a book-style foldable iPhone. Cote-Colisson also expects a special 20th-anniversary iPhone edition and smart glasses in 2027. “Combined with better AI, this could trigger a strong renewal cycle for users of iPhone series 15 and 16 (2023 2024),” Cote-Colisson summed up.

Cote-Colisson now joins 18 other analysts in the AAPL bull camp, while an additional 9 Holds and 2 Sells all add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. However, the $328.69 average price target suggests the shares will stay rangebound for the time being. (See Apple stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘The Uncertainty Lies Here,’ Says Investor About Micron Stock
data.description:
Micron (NASDAQ:MU) has been one of the standout performers in the semiconductor sector over the past year, delivering exceptional gains for shareholders. However, like many of the AI-driven chip stocks that surged during the rally, the shares have come under heavy selling pressure, falling ~30% since the company’s blockbuster earnings report on June 25.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

The decline has intensified this week despite a largely supportive industry backdrop. Semiconductor equipment leader ASML Holding raised its outlook earlier in the week, while Taiwan Semiconductor delivered better-than-expected results and increased its guidance, with both companies reinforcing the strength of AI-related demand.

Even so, a wave of profit-taking and several negative headlines have dented investor confidence.

According to CNBC, concerns include a proposed moratorium on data-center construction in New York State and reports that CoreWeave has explored hedging against potential future declines in memory prices. Both developments have added to the cautious sentiment surrounding AI infrastructure investments.

Investor Ragmar Rikberg, however, views these developments as “normal headwinds” rather than signs of a fundamental shift. He believes concerns over excessive AI spending and slower-than-expected monetization have been recurring themes for some time, while sharp gains across the sector have naturally encouraged investors to lock in profits.

“To me,” added the investor, “staying the course during periods of high volatility with long‑term positions means digging into the details to see whether the numbers still support the narrative. In Micron’s case—and for the memory sector more broadly—I don’t see the narrative turning negative; if anything, based on fresh commentary from ASML and TSMC, it looks even more supportive.”

That said, if that all sounds like the words of an MU bull, that’s not quite the case, as Rikberg has just downgraded his MU rating from Buy to Hold. (To watch Rikberg’s track record, click here)

While he believes the stock remains attractively valued, trading at roughly 5.5 times projected 2028 earnings, he argues that uncertainty surrounding future memory pricing limits his conviction.

Rikberg contends that the sector’s recent earnings growth has been fueled primarily by substantial price increases rather than volume alone, a dynamic affecting the entire memory industry rather than Micron specifically. He notes that major AI hardware suppliers, led by Nvidia, have benefited from an environment where gross margins above 80% have become commonplace, well above the semiconductor industry’s historical 30% to 60% range. Although those margins remain supported by a significant supply-demand imbalance, he believes that imbalance will eventually normalize. Unlike Nvidia, whose technological leadership and CUDA ecosystem provide a durable competitive advantage, memory manufacturers compete with “several similarly capable” rivals and are therefore more vulnerable to future pricing pressure.

“Whether more balanced pricing discussions in the memory market begin in 2028, 2030, or 2033, nobody knows—but at some point they inevitably will. And that’s a major uncertainty with no clear answers,” Rikberg summed up.

Rikberg’s cautious stance remains a minority view on Wall Street. While one analyst stays on the sidelines, all 29 other recent reviews are positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. At $1,569.29, the average price target offers 12-month upside of 84%. (See MU stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Alphabet Stock’s Earnings Are Coming – Here’s What Bank of America Expects
data.description:
Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is set to kick off the big tech earnings season next week, becoming the first major internet company to report second-quarter results. The search giant is scheduled to release its Q2 report after market close on Wednesday, April 22, with investors looking for insights into advertising trends across the sector, cloud momentum, and the outlook for AI-related capital spending.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

Looking ahead to the print, BofA’s Justin Post, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, expects Alphabet to deliver Q2 revenue of $102.1 billion and GAAP EPS of $8.38, ahead of Wall Street estimates of $101.0 billion and $2.90, respectively (the discrepancy in EPS is due to an estimated $80 billion operating income benefit from the revaluation of its Anthropic stake).  

Post forecasts Search revenue growth of 17.3% year-over-year, broadly in line with consensus expectations, while Google Cloud growth is expected to reach 70%, above the Street’s 65% forecast. For Search, third-party data and industry checks point to continued strength, particularly in retail advertising, although some softness in travel and consumer packaged goods could partially offset gains.

Google Cloud will obviously be a key focus. Post believes Google’s ongoing capacity expansion should support strong revenue growth as the company works to meet elevated demand. Recent developments – including Alphabet’s capital raise, reports that Google is limiting capacity availability for certain customers, and Amazon’s cloud pricing increases – are positive signals for cloud demand. Post now expects Google Cloud revenue to grow by $3.2 billion sequentially in Q2, compared with $2.3 billion of sequential growth in Q1, noting that the second quarter has historically been a stronger period for sequential growth.  

Post also expects Alphabet to signal that capital spending will continue to rise significantly in 2027, potentially leading Wall Street to raise forecasts. He noted that buy-side expectations are likely already running ahead of current sell-side estimates.

As for items to watch on the earnings call, Post expects investors to focus on several key areas, including the reasons behind Alphabet’s recent capital raise, future free cash flow expectations, confidence in returns from large-scale AI investments, and how additional capacity will be allocated between Google’s core businesses and Cloud.

Other likely discussion points include the drivers behind Google’s “expansionary moment” commentary around Search, AI-related corporate expense growth, the delay of the public launch of Gemini 3.5 Pro and the outlook for future model releases, TPU sales and monetization, and the loss of high-profile AI talent.

All told, Post remains squarely in Alphabet’s corner. “We continue to see Alphabet well positioned to drive outsized growth & Cloud margin upside given favorable AI positioning across models, silicon, consumer & enterprise distribution,” the 5-star analyst summed up.

To this end, Post reiterated a Buy rating on the shares, backed by a price objective of $430, a figure pointing toward 12-month returns of 16%. (To watch Post’s track record, click here)

28 other analysts join Post in the GOOGL bull camp, while an additional 5 Holds can’t detract from a Strong Buy consensus rating. At $435.78, the average target implies shares will gain 17.5% in the months ahead. (See GOOGL stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: KeyBanc Raises Amazon Stock’s Price Target Ahead of Earnings: Here’s What to Watch
data.description:
We are currently seeing a rotation out of high-flying chip stocks into lagging tech giants. Against this backdrop, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock has been edging higher in recent sessions, with shares up by 12% since late June.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

AMZO: built for a short position on AMZN

It’s too early to tell if this will be a longer-lasting trend, but in any case, for KeyBanc analyst Justin Patterson, the shares have plenty of room left to run.

In fact, ahead of Amazon’s Q2 earnings on July 30, Patterson has raised his AMZN price target from $330 to $335, implying the stock will gain 31% over the next year. Patterson’s rating remains Overweight (i.e., Buy).

“AMZN remains our preferred e-commerce stock as: 1) underlying retail trends remain strong; and 2) AWS is inflecting,” the analyst said. “While investment in Leo and data centers may limit NT margin upside, we believe consensus is understating revenue and EPS into 2028E (we are 2% and 3% above Street).”  (To watch Patterson’s track record, click here)

Patterson highlights several key themes to watch ahead of the results.

AWS growth remains the most important driver of sentiment as investors assess returns on the company’s massive AI infrastructure buildout. The analyst expects AWS sales growth to accelerate in Q2, supported by strong core demand, expanded partnerships with OpenAI and Anthropic, and AI-related offerings such as custom chips and Bedrock. AWS backlog is expected to reach around $485 billion, largely driven by Anthropic’s $100 billion, 10-year agreement, although the deal may extend backlog duration. Patterson will also be watching Trainium adoption, with new generations potentially providing an additional growth catalyst.

Capex remains another major focus. Amazon has indicated that faster AWS growth requires increased infrastructure spending, while long-term commitments from OpenAI and Anthropic will require additional capacity. He forecasts capex of $331 billion in 2027 and $356 billion in 2028, well above Wall Street estimates of $235 billion and $241 billion. Despite concerns about AI infrastructure overbuilding, Patterson believes that demand for incremental compute capacity supports continued investment.

Advertising and agentic commerce are also emerging as important growth areas. Amazon’s integration of Rufus and Alexa+ into Alexa for Shopping created a unified AI shopping experience, while new agentic advertising formats suggest the company is positioning itself for AI-driven commerce. Although Patterson sees debate on whether AI agents reduce direct customer journeys on Amazon, he sees “sustainable value in pricing, speed, and personalization in an agentic world.”

Meanwhile, Amazon’s Leo satellite initiative reached a milestone in July, expanding the constellation enough to begin commercialization. This is an “important milestone” that allows Amazon to capitalize some Leo costs rather than expensing them immediately, potentially creating smoother operating expense growth, though further investment will likely be required to compete with SpaceX’s Starlink.

AMZN stock gets plenty of support elsewhere on the Street. Based on a total of 45 Buys and 1 Hold, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating. At $318.98, the average price target implies shares will climb 25% higher in the months ahead. (See AMZN stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: One Hike, One Cut: Analysts Take Different Actions on Microsoft Stock Ahead of Earnings
data.description:
Microsoft (NASDAQ: MSFT) investors are anxiously awaiting the tech giant’s upcoming fiscal fourth quarter (June quarter) results, hoping the readout could help shift sentiment.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

The stock has been a laggard in 2026, showing year-to-date losses of 20%. Investors remain concerned that Microsoft’s massive AI infrastructure investments may not deliver the expected returns, while the potential impact of AI on traditional software offerings has also become a big focal point.

The key question is whether the stock deserves to stay in the penalty box or whether it currently offers a favorable risk/reward opportunity.

Assessing the company’s prospects, Evercore ISI analyst Kirk Materne believes the business remains on “strong footing,” with revenue and operating income expected to expand at double-digit rates in fiscal 2027.

However, he argues that the stock has struggled to gain traction because growth-focused investors want evidence of accelerating Azure revenue and stronger Copilot monetization and adoption. Meanwhile, value investors are looking for greater clarity on when free cash flow could reach a bottom and reassurance that rising gross margin pressures will not “negatively impact EPS expectations.”

Given that he expects Microsoft to continue pursuing an aggressive AI investment strategy, he does not view the FQ4 results as a “major inflection point” in the ongoing discussion around capital expenditure levels. Materne believes consensus estimates remain somewhat conservative, with the Street forecasting roughly $180 billion in FY27 cash capex compared with Evercore’s estimate of approximately $210 billion.

That said, the analyst believes that accelerating Azure growth in the second half of the year and further progress in Copilot monetization could help the shares “build some momentum,” particularly as capital expenditure growth might begin to normalize in 2027.

“Bottom line,” the analyst summed up, “We believe that the risk/reward continues to skew to the upside and if MSFT can provide some additional clarity on when FCF could inflect, the upside optionality is material. In our view, sentiment is overly apathetic at current levels, and we see an attractive catalyst path for MSFT in 2H CY26 with Azure acceleration on tap, Copilot momentum starting to build, and capex growth likely to peak in the coming quarters.”

Accordingly, Materne maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, and raised his price target from $510 to $525, implying the stock will gain 36% over the coming months. (To watch Materne’s track record, click here)

Looking ahead to the print, Wells Fargo analyst Michael Turrin sees a mixed setup, with investors still focused on questions surrounding Microsoft’s capital intensity and cloud market share. An outsized Azure beat could help ease some concerns; otherwise, he expects the broader debate to continue. However, he believes several factors could support stronger performance in fiscal 2027, including Azure reacceleration, growth in first-party model development and Copilot adoption, and continued operating expense discipline.

Turrin does not expect a meaningful change to Microsoft’s 2026 capex outlook, which already factors in roughly $25 billion of cost inflation. However, he expects infrastructure costs to continue rising, maintaining his FY27 capex estimate at $226 billion while increasing his FY28 forecast by 13% to $278 billion.

Turrin also remains an MSFT bull, reiterating an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the stock. However, his price target goes from $650 to $625. Still, the new target makes room for 12-month returns of 62%. (To watch Turrin’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the stock claims an additional 33 Buys and 1 Hold, for a Strong Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for one-year returns of 45%, considering the average target clocks in at $559.14. (See MSFT stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Bulls on Hold: Oppenheimer Downgrades IBM Stock – Here’s Why
data.description:
One good thing IBM (NYSE:IBM) investors can take into Wednesday’s trading is that it surely can’t get worse than Tuesday. The stock’s historic 25% drop was its steepest ever, erasing ~$67 billion in market cap.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

High conviction IBM bulls now have this Tradr ETF

The decline was driven by a weaker-than-expected preliminary earnings update, with results missing consensus estimates across every segment. Additionally, a letter from CEO Arvind Krishna spooked investors, with Krishna acknowledging that the company had not moved quickly enough to respond to customers unexpectedly shifting their spending priorities from software toward AI infrastructure and hardware.

More specifically, management cited several factors, including softer demand for Transaction Processing software (the software stack associated with mainframe systems), customers redirecting spending toward servers and storage amid accelerated purchases related to rising memory costs, and delays in closing large contracts before the quarter ended.

There’s no two ways about it, says Oppenheimer analyst Param Singh, IBM’s pre-announcement was “disappointing,” with results falling short of expectations, especially in software.

The company delivered Q2 software revenue growth of 5% year-over-year, significantly below Singh’s call for 12% growth.

While there were a “few bright spots” – including Red Hat accelerating to 11% sequential growth, ongoing momentum in HashiCorp and Confluent, and continued strength in distributed infrastructure – Singh believes the company will struggle to achieve its full-year guidance or deliver “double-digit” constant-currency software growth in 2026/27 without further major acquisitions or a “material catch-up” in large deals that were delayed during Q2.

The weakness also spread across other software and consulting stocks, with several names experiencing declines. Looking at the broader implications, Singh expects the shift in spending toward hardware to be beneficial for server and storage vendors, including Everpure and NetApp within his coverage. However, he sees “near-term risks” for infrastructure software providers, which may face similar pressure from tighter IT budgets. Nutanix could be one such vendor exposed to these headwinds. Security software appears to be holding up well, and he doesn’t think any meaningful risk applies to Cloudflare, Akamai, Commvault, or Rubrik.

“The bull thesis will take longer to materialize, and we anticipate the stock will be range bound near term,” the analyst summed up.

Accordingly, Singh has downgraded his IBM rating from Outperform (i.e., Buy) to Perform (Neutral). (To watch Singh’s track record, click here)

4 other analysts are currently on the IBM fence, while an additional 11 Buys and 1 Sell all add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. Meanwhile, the $302 average price target implies shares will climb 39% higher in the months ahead. (See IBM stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: IBM Stock Is Having Its Darkest Day Ever – It’s About to Get Worse, According to HSBC
data.description:
It looks like Tuesday will be one for the record books for IBM (NYSE:IBM), though not for reasons shareholders will welcome. At the time of writing, the stock has plunged 25.5%, putting it on course for its worst single-day decline ever. The previous record came on October 19, 1987 – Black Monday – when the shares shed 23.7%, matching one of the darkest days in stock market history.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

High conviction IBM bulls now have this Tradr ETF

The massive drop came off the back of the legacy tech company’spreliminary second-quarter results, which fell shy of expectations. IBM said revenue hit $17.2 billion, missing the consensus estimate of $17.85 billion, while adjusted EPS came in at $2.93 vs. the $3.02 analysts were looking for.

The weakness also rippled across software and consulting names after CEO Arvind Krishna said customers had rapidly redirected IT spending toward AI infrastructure. According to Krishna, demand for servers, storage, and memory to support AI data centers disrupted business in a way that “impacted client buying patterns.”

So, might it be time for investors to wade in and “buy the dip”? Quite the opposite appears to be the message in HSBC analyst Abhishek Shukla’s latest note. In fact, Shukla is bound to think investors’ actions are entirely apt, given the analyst has downgraded his IBM rating from Hold (i.e., Neutral) to Reduce (Sell) and lowered his price target from $231 to $191, implying the stock has further downside of 11.5% in it. (To watch Shukla’s track record, click here)

Shukla’s bearish stance is based on the idea investors can build a “synthetic IBM” from a basket of peers that offers “meaningfully better medium-term earnings power vs IBM.”

Based on last week’s closing price of $287.56, an investor could instead purchase roughly 1.19 shares of IonQ, 1.22 shares of SAP, 0.16 shares of Accenture, and 0.95 shares of HP.

While Tuesday’s bloodbath means the stock has since lost nearly a quarter of its value, Shukla estimates that portfolio would generate 2030 EPS of $23.15, around 40% above IBM’s projected non-GAAP EPS of $16.59, while maintaining “broadly similar subsector exposure.” Quantum computing represents about 18% of the synthetic portfolio’s initial investment, similar to its estimated contribution to IBM’s enterprise value.

Talking of quantum computing, Shukla also believes IonQ might be “stealing a march over IBM’s quantum business.” Over the past five quarters, IBM appears to have secured roughly $100 million in new quantum orders, compared with nearly $600 million for IonQ. Even so, IBM still leads in cumulative lifetime orders, with more than $1.1 billion versus an estimated $789 million for IonQ.

Meanwhile, the analyst also argues that SAP and Accenture offer more durable growth prospects, whereas IBM’s expansion remains heavily reliant on ongoing cost-cutting initiatives.

Turning now to the rest of the Street, where Shukla is actually the lone IBM bear, with an additional 12 Buys and 5 Holds all adding up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. At $303.83, the average price target implies the stock will gain 41% over the coming months. (See IBM stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Walk Away’: Why KeyBanc Has Now Turned Bearish on Apple Stock
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) stock saw out Monday’s trading notching a new all-time high, following reports that the tech giant might be bucking the trend. iPhone shipments increased by 3% on a year-over-year basis in Q2, allowing Apple to capture a record 20% share of global smartphone shipments despite an industry-wide decline. Overall market shipments fell as the memory shortage drove up component costs, putting pressure on lower-priced devices.

TipRanks Welcomes a New ETF – NYSE:RANK

  • TipRanks has entered a new arena in the investing world, powering the index of an ETF based on its unique data now trading under the ticker RANK on the NYSE.
  • RANK tracks the performance of the TipRanks US Momentum Analysts Index, a rules-based index of 50 large U.S. companies.

However, if that sounds like a bullish setup for Apple, think again. With the stock now sitting at elevated levels, KeyBanc analyst Brandon Nispel believes the outlook for the tech giant is not as rosy as it is made out to be.

In fact, Nispel has now taken a bearish stance on Apple’s prospects, downgrading his rating from Sector Weight (i.e., Neutral) to Underweight (Sell). Nispel’s $250 price target implies the stock is overvalued by 21%. (To watch Nispel’s track record, click here)

While the stock got a boost from some promising data, Nispel’s findings tell a different story. The investment firm’s KFLD (Key First Look Data) suggests that U.S. demand is beginning to normalize after last year’s tariff-related pull-forward. Indexed spending declined 2% month-over-month in June, below the three-year average increase of 9%. For Q2, indexed spending fell 2.7% sequentially (albeit better than the three-year average of -3.7% QoQ) and 3% YoY, compared with a 6% YoY increase in Q1.

The analyst believes the weaker U.S. trend contrasts with Apple’s expected hardware growth, suggesting strength is likely coming from China and other international markets. Historically, Apple’s fiscal third-quarter hardware revenue declines nearly 10% sequentially, but consensus assumes a much smaller decline this year (-4.3%). The analyst argues current expectations are too optimistic given the underlying demand trends.

Looking at the bigger picture, Nispel raises several concerns. One being that U.S. carriers are reducing device subsidies, which could slow upgrade cycles, while international growth will need to offset weaker U.S. momentum. However, rising iPhone prices could make overseas growth more challenging. Nispel views consensus iPhone growth expectations of 8% for FY27 as too aggressive and sees risks to Mac and iPad estimates following recent price increases.  

Slower hardware unit growth could also pressure Services growth, which the analyst expects to decelerate to 7% in FY27 vs. Street expectations of around 12%. “With Apple trading at ~24.5x our FY27 EV/ EBITDA and ~35x P/E, we think the stock is overvalued relative to history and see its 2+ standard deviation premium to the S&P 500/Nasdaq as unwarranted,” Nispel summed up.

So, that’s the KeyBanc view, but what does the rest of the Street have in mind for Apple? One analyst joins Nispel in the bear camp, but with an additional 18 Buys and 10 Holds, the stock claims a Moderate Buy consensus rating. Going by the $324.23 average price target, the shares will stay rangebound for the time being. (See Apple stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel
